---
title: "R Episode 7 — Control Flow"
---

::: {.callout-note}
## Objectives
By the end of this episode you will be able to:

- Branch with `if` / `else if` / `else`.
- Repeat with `for` loops.
- Recognize when vectorized or functional code is clearer than a loop.

**Teaching:** 30 min · **Exercises:** 20 min
:::

## Conditionals

```r
x <- 8
if (x > 10) {
  print("big")
} else if (x > 5) {
  print("medium")
} else {
  print("small")
}
```

`if` expects a **single** logical value. For element-wise choices on a vector, use the
vectorized `ifelse()` (or `dplyr::if_else()`):

```r
scores <- c(45, 82, 67, 91)
ifelse(scores >= 60, "pass", "fail")
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Instructor note
A very common error: passing a length-> 1 vector to `if`. Modern R raises an error (older R only
warned). Steer learners toward `ifelse()`/`if_else()` for vectors from the start.
:::

## Loops

```r
for (continent in unique(gapminder$continent)) {
  rows <- gapminder[gapminder$continent == continent, ]
  cat(continent, "mean lifeExp:", round(mean(rows$lifeExp), 1), "\n")
}
```

Loops are fine and readable — but in R, **vectorized** and **functional** approaches are often
clearer and faster (next episode).

## Prefer vectorized / functional style

The loop above is more idiomatic as a grouped summary (Episode 12):

```r
library(dplyr)
gapminder |>
  group_by(continent) |>
  summarise(mean_life = mean(lifeExp))
```

…or with `purrr::map_dbl()` when you need a function applied over a list/vector.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Challenge — Rewrite without a loop
This loop classifies values; rewrite it as one vectorized call.

```r
out <- character(length(scores))
for (i in seq_along(scores)) {
  out[i] <- if (scores[i] >= 60) "pass" else "fail"
}
```

### Solution

```r
out <- ifelse(scores >= 60, "pass", "fail")
```
Shorter, faster, and far less error-prone than the index-based loop.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Key points
- `if` takes a single logical; use `ifelse()`/`if_else()` for vectors.
- `for` loops work, but vectorized/functional code is usually clearer in R.
- Reach for grouped `dplyr` summaries or `purrr::map` before writing index loops.
:::

Next: [Episode 8 — Publication-Quality Graphics with ggplot2](r-08-ggplot2.qmd).
